questions.questionMd: When using `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia()` with a Promise, how is the camera stream accessed?

options.text: By reading the return value of the function directly
By listening for a DOM event fired when the camera opens
By passing an onSuccess callback as the second argument
By attaching a .then() handler that receives the stream as its argument

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "deserialize" a string?

options.text: To convert a JavaScript object into a JSON string
To turn a serialized string back into a JavaScript object
To split a string into an array using a separator
To encode a string using HTML entities

questions.questionMd: Why does calling `fetch()` on a local file opened via `file://` protocol result in an error?

options.text: JavaScript blocks all file I/O on the main thread
Local files must be fetched using XMLHttpRequest instead
The fetch API only supports JSON files, not text files
Loading files via the file:// protocol is disallowed by the browser

questions.questionMd: Why is storing data as JSON preferable to storing it as plain text when using `fetch()`?

options.text: JSON files load faster because they are compressed by the browser
Plain text requires XMLHttpRequest while JSON works with fetch
JSON is the only format the Fetch API is able to read
JSON eliminates the need to manually parse field separators when deserializing structured data

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string?

options.text: JSON.parse()
JSON.deserialize()
JSON.stringify()
JSON.encode()

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` takes a JavaScript object and transforms it into a string format.

questions.questionMd: What is the key difference between `navigator.getUserMedia` and `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia`?

options.text: The deprecated version uses Promises; the newer version uses callbacks
The newer version returns a Promise; the deprecated version uses callbacks
The newer version requires HTTPS while the deprecated version does not
Only the deprecated version supports video streaming
JavaScript Object Notation
JavaScript Syntax Object Notation
JavaScript Ordered Node
Java Serialized Object Network

questions.questionMd: Which method on a fetch `response` object provides built-in support for parsing JSON directly?

options.text: response.text()
response.json()
response.parse()
response.decode()

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly rewrites `fetch('f.txt').then(onResponse)` using an intermediate variable?

options.text: const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.resolve(onResponse);
let p = onResponse; p.fetch('f.txt');
const p = new Promise('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);
const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);

questions.questionMd: In the completed fetch example, which JavaScript method is used to break the fetched text into individual image URLs?

options.text: text.join(' ')
text.replace(' ', ',')
text.slice(' ')
text.split(' ')

questions.questionMd: When chaining `.then()` calls on a fetch Promise, what does the second `.then()` receive as its argument?

options.text: The value returned or resolved by the first .then() handler
The HTTP status code from the first .then()
The original fetch response object
A new fetch Promise targeting the same resource

questions.questionMd: What does `response.status` contain when used inside a fetch success handler?

options.text: A boolean indicating whether the request succeeded
The HTTP status code of the request, such as 200
The text content of the fetched resource
The name of the file that was fetched

questions.questionMd: How does `response.text()` read the response data according to the lecture?

options.text: Synchronously, by returning the string directly
Asynchronously, by returning a Promise that resolves with the response string
By dispatching a DOM event when reading is complete
By writing the result into a global variable

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which statement best captures the relationship between using and defining Promises?

options.text: Promises are easier to define than to use
Promises must be fully understood internally before they can be used
Promises are easier to use than to define
Promises are equally straightforward to both define and use

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, the Fetch API is made up of how many functions?

options.text: Two
Three
One
Four

questions.explanationMd: The core API consists of a single global `fetch()` function.
